title: Appendix A — Topic
text:
This user guide will cover the basic use of autoencoders for dimensionality reduction. It will explain what autoencoders do, how they compress high dimensional data into a lower dimensional representation, and how this differs from traditional methods like PCA. The focus is on practical use rather than theory, with attention to choosing an appropriate bottleneck size, training the model using reconstruction loss, and understanding the output as a learned feature representation.
The guide will reference common software packages such as Keras and Torch, with PCA from R’s prcomp used as a comparison baseline. It will discuss when to use autoencoders and when not to use them. By the end, users will know how to apply an autoencoder at a high level.






Comments/suggestions



This sounds great – make sure you use lots of pictures to show the dimensionality reduction relative to PCA – you may also want to compare to t-SNE and other nonlinear dimension reduction techniques. You might look up some work by Di Cook and Jayani Lackshika (spelling on the last name is ???) on visualizing multidimensional data that has been run through dimension reduction tools.
Be careful with setting up Keras/Torch reproducibly - remember that code has to run on my machine, not just yours. So your user guide will need to be very detailed as far as the python environment setup. More detailed than most python packages… sigh.
crumbs: Appendices
A  Topic

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians
text:
2 Introduction
High-dimensional data (data with many columns) is common in fields like biology, medicine, and engineering. When data has too many columns, models become slow, visualization becomes impossible, and noise drowns out meaningful patterns. An autoencoder is a neural network that compresses your data automatically, keeping the important structure and discarding the rest. This makes autoencoders useful for visualization, noise reduction, and finding hidden patterns that simpler methods like PCA miss.
This guide walks you through building and using an autoencoder from start to finish using a real dataset you can run yourself.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians

title: Appendix B — Needs Assessment
text:
B.0.0.1 What does someone trying to accomplish your chosen task need help with?
A statistician familiar withPCA but new to deep learning will need help understanding what an autoencoder does, how to build and train one, and how to extract the compressed representation for downstream use.


B.0.0.2 What parts are likely to be tricky?
There is no equivalent to a scree plot for choosing the bottleneck size, and the compressed dimensions have no direct interpretation. Knowing when to stop training and how to extract just the encoder after training are also difficult parts.


B.0.0.3 What resources are already available on this topic that may be helpful? Look for e.g. software vignettes, package documentation, papers about software packages, and so on.
The paper cites Goodfellow et al. (2016) Deep Learning and Murphy (2022) Probabilistic Machine Learning as general references. Doersch (2021) “Tutorial on Variational Autoencoders” is a useful starting point for autoencoders specifically. Keras and PyTorch both have official documentation with working examples, and R’s prcomp documentation provides a helpful PCA baseline for comparison.






Note



I highly recommend finding some blog posts and other online resources that break this down in a non-academic fashion to serve as additional resources. Ideally things that don’t run only in a Jupyter notebook (personal bias – I can never replicate those things).
crumbs: Appendices
B  Needs Assessment

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
3.1 What PCA Does
PCA finds a set of orthogonal linear directions — principal components — that capture the maximum variance in your data. If your data matrix \(X\) has \(p\) columns (features), PCA finds a projection \(Z = XW\) where \(W\) is a \(p \times k\) matrix of loadings and \(k \ll p\). You recover an approximation of the original data as \(\hat{X} = ZW^\top\).
This is a two-step process:

Encode: \(Z = XW\) (compress from \(p\) to \(k\) dimensions)
Decode: \(\hat{X} = ZW^\top\) (reconstruct from \(k\) back to \(p\) dimensions)

The quality of the reconstruction is measured by how much variance is retained, which is why the scree plot — showing variance explained by each component — is the standard diagnostic for choosing \(k\).
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
3.2 What an Autoencoder Does
An autoencoder does the same two steps, but replaces the linear projection with neural networks that can learn nonlinear transformations:

Encoder: A neural network \(f_\theta\) that maps input \(x \in \mathbb{R}^p\) to a compressed representation \(z \in \mathbb{R}^k\)
Decoder: A neural network \(g_\phi\) that maps \(z\) back to a reconstruction \(\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^p\)

The network is trained end-to-end by minimizing reconstruction loss — how different \(\hat{x}\) is from \(x\). No labels are needed; the input is its own target.
Original data (p features)
        │
   ┌────▼─────┐
   │ ENCODER  │  ← neural network layers, getting narrower
   └────┬─────┘
        │
   ┌────▼─────┐
   │BOTTLENECK│  ← k dimensions (k << p); this is your compressed representation
   └────┬─────┘
        │
   ┌────▼─────┐
   │ DECODER  │  ← neural network layers, getting wider again
   └────┬─────┘
        │
Reconstructed data (p features)
Because the network must pass information through a narrow bottleneck, it is forced to learn a compact representation of the data’s most important structure. Unlike PCA, this structure can be nonlinear.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
section: 3.3 How Autoencoders Compare to PCA and t-SNE
text:
3.3 How Autoencoders Compare to PCA and t-SNE
There are several dimensionality reduction methods available, each with different strengths. Table 3.1 summarizes the most commonly used ones.



Table 3.1: Comparison of dimensionality reduction methods





Property
PCA
t-SNE
Autoencoder




Transformation type
Linear
Nonlinear
Nonlinear


Axes are interpretable
Yes (variance %)
No
No


Handles complex structure
No
Yes
Yes


Good for downstream modeling
Yes
Not recommended
Yes


Good for 2D/3D visualization
Yes
Excellent
Possible


Requires training
No
No
Yes


Reproducible by default
Yes
No (stochastic)
Yes (with seeds)


Easy to tune
Yes (scree plot)
Moderate
Harder


Works on images/audio/text
Poor
Possible
Excellent






The key trade-off is this: PCA is fast, interpretable, and requires no training, but it can only capture linear structure. t-SNE is excellent for visualization but its output cannot be used in a downstream model. Autoencoders sit in between — they can capture nonlinear structure and their output is useful for modeling, but they require more setup, more data, and more tuning.






A useful intuition



Think of PCA as finding the best flat shadow of your data on a lower-dimensional plane. An autoencoder learns a curved surface to project onto — one shaped to your specific dataset. For data with genuinely nonlinear structure (images, time series, gene expression), this matters a great deal. For data that is mostly linear, PCA will often do just as well with far less effort.


Work by (cook2018?) and colleagues on interactive visualization of high-dimensional data suggests that examining the reduced representation visually — rotating it, coloring by known groups — is an important step in evaluating any dimensionality reduction method, autoencoder or otherwise.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
4.1 Option A: PyTorch Setup (Recommended)
PyTorch is generally preferred for research and custom architectures. It uses an imperative (eager) execution model that is easier to debug.

4.1.1 Step 1: Create an isolated environment
# Using conda (recommended for managing CUDA dependencies)
conda create -n autoencoder-env python=3.10
conda activate autoencoder-env
Or using venv:
python3.10 -m venv autoencoder-env
source autoencoder-env/bin/activate   # Mac/Linux
autoencoder-env\Scripts\activate      # Windows


4.1.2 Step 2: Install PyTorch
Go to https://pytorch.org/get-started/locally/ and use the selector to get the exact install command for your OS and hardware. For CPU-only (no GPU):
pip install torch==2.2.0 torchvision==0.17.0 --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
For CUDA 11.8 (NVIDIA GPU):
pip install torch==2.2.0 torchvision==0.17.0 --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118


4.1.3 Step 3: Install supporting packages
pip install numpy==1.26.4 pandas==2.2.1 scikit-learn==1.4.1 matplotlib==3.8.3 seaborn==0.13.2


4.1.4 Step 4: Pin your environment
pip freeze > requirements.txt
Anyone reproducing your work installs with:
pip install -r requirements.txt


4.1.5 Step 5: Set random seeds
Neural networks involve randomness at multiple points: weight initialization, mini-batch sampling, and (on GPUs) non-deterministic operations. To make results reproducible, set seeds at the top of every script:
import random
import numpy as np
import torch

SEED = 42

random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)
torch.manual_seed(SEED)
torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(SEED)

# For full determinism on GPU (may slow training slightly)
torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True
torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
4.2 Option B: Keras (TensorFlow backend) Setup
Keras provides a higher-level API that is often easier for those coming from R or scikit-learn.

4.2.1 Step 1: Create environment
conda create -n autoencoder-keras python=3.10
conda activate autoencoder-keras


4.2.2 Step 2: Install TensorFlow and Keras
pip install tensorflow==2.15.0
pip install numpy==1.26.4 pandas==2.2.1 scikit-learn==1.4.1 matplotlib==3.8.3






TensorFlow on Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3 Macs)



Standard TensorFlow does not run natively on Apple Silicon. Use the Apple-maintained fork instead:
pip install tensorflow-macos==2.15.0
pip install tensorflow-metal  # enables GPU acceleration




4.2.3 Step 3: Set random seeds in Keras
import os
import random
import numpy as np
import tensorflow as tf

SEED = 42

os.environ['PYTHONHASHSEED'] = str(SEED)
random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)
tf.random.set_seed(SEED)






Full reproducibility on GPU is not guaranteed



Even with all seeds set, GPU operations may be non-deterministic due to parallel floating-point operations. If exact reproducibility is critical, run on CPU or accept that results may vary slightly across runs. Document which hardware you used.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
4.3 Verifying Your Installation
Run the following to confirm everything works:
# PyTorch check
import torch
print(torch.__version__)
print("GPU available:", torch.cuda.is_available())

# Keras/TF check
import tensorflow as tf
print(tf.__version__)
print("GPU devices:", tf.config.list_physical_devices('GPU'))
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
section: 5.1 What Your Data Must Look Like
text:
5.1 What Your Data Must Look Like
Autoencoders have specific data requirements that differ somewhat from PCA:

Numeric only: All inputs must be numbers. Categorical variables must be encoded first (one-hot or ordinal encoding, depending on context).
No missing values: Neural networks cannot handle NaN. Impute missing values before training. Simple mean/median imputation is fine for getting started; more sophisticated methods (e.g., multiple imputation) are worth considering for high-missingness data.
Normalized inputs: This is critical. Neural networks are sensitive to the scale of inputs in a way that PCA (which centers data) is not. Features on very different scales will cause gradients to be dominated by large-magnitude features.
Enough data: As a rough rule of thumb, autoencoders are rarely worth the effort for datasets with fewer than a few thousand rows. For small data, PCA will match or beat an autoencoder’s performance.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
5.2 Normalization
The standard approach is min-max scaling to [0, 1] or standardization to zero mean and unit variance. The right choice depends on your output activation:

If your decoder’s output layer uses sigmoid activation, scale inputs to [0, 1] with min-max scaling
If your decoder’s output layer uses linear activation, use standardization (zero mean, unit variance)







Fit the scaler on training data only



A common mistake is to normalize using the full dataset before splitting into train/validation. This leaks information from the validation set into training. Always fit your scaler on training data, then apply it to both train and validation sets.


from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import pandas as pd

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv("your_data.csv")
X = df.values  # assumes all columns are numeric

# Split first, then normalize
X_train, X_val = train_test_split(X, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)

# Fit scaler on training data only
scaler = MinMaxScaler()
X_train = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_val = scaler.transform(X_val)  # apply (don't refit) to validation
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
6.1 Choosing an Architecture
An autoencoder has three parts: the encoder, the bottleneck, and the decoder. The encoder and decoder are typically mirror images of each other.

6.1.1 The bottleneck size
Choosing the bottleneck dimension \(k\) is the central design decision, and it is harder than choosing the number of PCA components. With PCA, the scree plot gives you a natural elbow to look for. With autoencoders, there is no equivalent diagnostic — you must choose \(k\) based on reasoning about the problem and adjust based on reconstruction quality.
Some practical guidance:

Start with domain knowledge: If you expect 5–10 meaningful latent factors in your data, start with a bottleneck around that size.
Try several values: Train autoencoders with \(k \in \{2, 4, 8, 16, \ldots\}\) and compare reconstruction loss on the validation set.
Consider the downstream task: If you plan to cluster in the latent space, 2–10 dimensions is usually easier to work with. If you are feeding the representation into a predictive model, more dimensions are acceptable.
Reconstruction loss alone is not sufficient: A model can have very low reconstruction loss while producing a useless latent space (e.g., by memorizing). Always evaluate the representation qualitatively.



6.1.2 Layer sizes
A common starting architecture uses a geometric progression of layer sizes between the input dimension and the bottleneck. For input dimension \(p = 100\) and bottleneck \(k = 8\):
Input (100) → 64 → 32 → 16 → Bottleneck (8) → 16 → 32 → 64 → Output (100)
There is no universal rule here. Wider and deeper networks can capture more complex structure but are harder to train and more prone to overfitting.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
6.2 Implementation in PyTorch
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class Autoencoder(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, bottleneck_dim):
        super().__init__()

        self.encoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(input_dim, 64),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(64, 32),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(32, bottleneck_dim)
        )

        self.decoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(bottleneck_dim, 32),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(32, 64),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(64, input_dim),
            nn.Sigmoid()   # use Sigmoid if inputs are scaled to [0,1]
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        z = self.encoder(x)
        x_hat = self.decoder(z)
        return x_hat

    def encode(self, x):
        """Call this to get just the compressed representation."""
        return self.encoder(x)






Why ReLU?



The ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) activation function — \(f(x) = \max(0, x)\) — is the default choice for hidden layers in modern networks. It is computationally cheap and does not suffer from the vanishing gradient problem that affects sigmoid activations in deep networks. The output layer activation is different: use Sigmoid for [0,1] outputs or no activation (linear) for unbounded outputs.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
6.3 Implementation in Keras
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow import keras
from tensorflow.keras import layers

def build_autoencoder(input_dim, bottleneck_dim):
    # --- Encoder ---
    inputs = keras.Input(shape=(input_dim,))
    x = layers.Dense(64, activation="relu")(inputs)
    x = layers.Dense(32, activation="relu")(x)
    z = layers.Dense(bottleneck_dim, name="bottleneck")(x)

    # --- Decoder ---
    x = layers.Dense(32, activation="relu")(z)
    x = layers.Dense(64, activation="relu")(x)
    outputs = layers.Dense(input_dim, activation="sigmoid")(x)

    # Full autoencoder
    autoencoder = keras.Model(inputs, outputs, name="autoencoder")

    # Encoder only (for extracting representations)
    encoder = keras.Model(inputs, z, name="encoder")

    return autoencoder, encoder

autoencoder, encoder = build_autoencoder(input_dim=100, bottleneck_dim=8)
autoencoder.summary()
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
7.1 Reconstruction Loss
The training objective is to minimize the difference between the input \(x\) and the reconstruction \(\hat{x}\). For continuous, normalized data the standard choice is mean squared error (MSE):
\[\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \|x_i - \hat{x}_i\|^2\]
For binary data (e.g., one-hot encoded features or pixel values treated as Bernoulli), binary cross-entropy is more appropriate:
\[\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ x_i \log \hat{x}_i + (1 - x_i) \log(1 - \hat{x}_i) \right]\]
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
7.2 Training Loop in PyTorch
import torch
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset

# Convert numpy arrays to PyTorch tensors
X_train_t = torch.tensor(X_train, dtype=torch.float32)
X_val_t   = torch.tensor(X_val,   dtype=torch.float32)

train_loader = DataLoader(TensorDataset(X_train_t), batch_size=64, shuffle=True)

# Initialize model, loss, optimizer
INPUT_DIM      = X_train.shape[1]
BOTTLENECK_DIM = 8
EPOCHS         = 100
LR             = 1e-3

model     = Autoencoder(INPUT_DIM, BOTTLENECK_DIM)
criterion = nn.MSELoss()
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=LR)

train_losses, val_losses = [], []

for epoch in range(EPOCHS):
    # --- Training ---
    model.train()
    epoch_loss = 0
    for (batch,) in train_loader:
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        recon = model(batch)
        loss  = criterion(recon, batch)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        epoch_loss += loss.item() * len(batch)
    train_losses.append(epoch_loss / len(X_train_t))

    # --- Validation ---
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        val_recon = model(X_val_t)
        val_loss  = criterion(val_recon, X_val_t).item()
    val_losses.append(val_loss)

    if (epoch + 1) % 10 == 0:
        print(f"Epoch {epoch+1:3d} | Train loss: {train_losses[-1]:.4f} | Val loss: {val_losses[-1]:.4f}")
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
7.3 Training in Keras
Keras handles most of the above automatically:
autoencoder, encoder = build_autoencoder(input_dim=X_train.shape[1], bottleneck_dim=8)
autoencoder.compile(optimizer="adam", loss="mse")

history = autoencoder.fit(
    X_train, X_train,                     # input = target (reconstruction)
    epochs=100,
    batch_size=64,
    validation_data=(X_val, X_val)
)
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
7.4 Monitoring Training
Always plot training and validation loss together. This is the closest thing to a diagnostic plot that autoencoders offer.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
plt.plot(train_losses, label="Train loss")
plt.plot(val_losses,   label="Validation loss")
plt.xlabel("Epoch")
plt.ylabel("Reconstruction loss (MSE)")
plt.title("Autoencoder training curves")
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

7.4.1 What to look for

Both losses decrease and converge: Training is going well.
Validation loss plateaus while training loss keeps falling: Overfitting. Add dropout, reduce model size, or stop early.
Both losses plateau very early at a high value: The model is underfitting. Try a larger architecture or more epochs.
Loss oscillates wildly: The learning rate is too high. Try reducing it (e.g., lr=1e-4).







Early stopping



Rather than guessing the right number of epochs, use early stopping: halt training when validation loss has not improved for some number of epochs (“patience”). This prevents overfitting and saves time.
PyTorch (manual):
best_val = float('inf')
patience, wait = 15, 0
for epoch in range(500):
    # ... training loop ...
    if val_loss < best_val:
        best_val = val_loss
        torch.save(model.state_dict(), "best_model.pt")
        wait = 0
    else:
        wait += 1
        if wait >= patience:
            print(f"Early stopping at epoch {epoch+1}")
            break
model.load_state_dict(torch.load("best_model.pt"))
Keras (built-in):
early_stop = keras.callbacks.EarlyStopping(
    monitor="val_loss", patience=15, restore_best_weights=True
)
history = autoencoder.fit(..., callbacks=[early_stop])
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
8.1 Extracting in PyTorch
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    X_train_encoded = model.encode(X_train_t).numpy()
    X_val_encoded   = model.encode(X_val_t).numpy()

print(X_train_encoded.shape)  # (n_train, bottleneck_dim)
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
8.2 Extracting in Keras
In Keras, we already built a separate encoder model during construction:
X_train_encoded = encoder.predict(X_train)
X_val_encoded   = encoder.predict(X_val)

print(X_train_encoded.shape)  # (n_train, bottleneck_dim)
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
8.3 Using the Representation Downstream
The encoded matrix can be used like any other feature matrix — for clustering, classification, regression, or visualization.
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# Clustering in latent space
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=5, random_state=42)
cluster_labels = kmeans.fit_predict(X_train_encoded)

# Classification using encoded features
clf = LogisticRegression()
clf.fit(X_train_encoded, y_train)
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
8.4 Visualizing the Latent Space
If your bottleneck is 2D, you can visualize directly. For higher dimensions, apply PCA or t-SNE on top of the encoded representation:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA

# Reduce to 2D for visualization (if bottleneck > 2)
pca_vis = PCA(n_components=2)
Z_2d = pca_vis.fit_transform(X_train_encoded)

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
plt.scatter(Z_2d[:, 0], Z_2d[:, 1], alpha=0.5, s=10)
plt.xlabel("PC1 of latent space")
plt.ylabel("PC2 of latent space")
plt.title("2D projection of autoencoder latent space")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()






The latent dimensions have no natural interpretation



Unlike PCA, where the first component explains the most variance, the dimensions of an autoencoder’s bottleneck have no inherent ordering or meaning. Two autoencoders trained on the same data with the same architecture may learn different latent representations that are equally valid. Downstream evaluation (does it help my clustering / prediction task?) is the right metric.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
9.1 Reconstruction loss curve
Train autoencoders with different bottleneck sizes and plot validation reconstruction loss as a function of \(k\):
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

bottleneck_sizes = [2, 4, 8, 16, 32]
val_losses_by_k  = {}

for k in bottleneck_sizes:
    model = Autoencoder(INPUT_DIM, k)
    # ... train model ...
    val_losses_by_k[k] = final_val_loss

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
plt.plot(list(val_losses_by_k.keys()), list(val_losses_by_k.values()), marker='o')
plt.xlabel("Bottleneck dimension (k)")
plt.ylabel("Validation reconstruction loss")
plt.title("Reconstruction loss vs. bottleneck size")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Look for an elbow in this curve — the point where increasing \(k\) yields diminishing returns in reconstruction quality. This is the closest analog to the PCA scree plot.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
9.2 Downstream task performance
If you have a downstream task (e.g., predicting an outcome, clustering samples), evaluate performance at each \(k\) using the encoded representations. Choose the smallest \(k\) that achieves acceptable downstream performance.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: User Guide: Annual Review Article Topic
text:
Assignment Flow
This project is set up to scaffold this user guide and set you up for success.
Start at topic.qmd and identify a topic for your user guide.
Once you’ve proposed a topic and received my approval/feedback, you can convert the contents of topic.qmd into an introductory paragraph in your report. Please leave the topic.qmd file as an appendix, to document the different stages of this project.
Once we’ve agreed on a topic for your user guide, you will proceed to needs.qmd and task-analysis.qmd. Both will be submitted at the same check-in, and both will be included in your final report as appendices. You should be able to re-purpose most of the information in task-analysis.qmd to orient the user to different components of the task you’ve chosen.
Your next step is to actually write the content that satisfies the work you outlined in task-analysis.qmd.
Once you’re finished with that, ideally, you’ll have plenty of time to edit and streamline your report. Feel free to trade reports with a friend and try to complete your friend’s task - this will help you both identify areas where the guide isn’t as clear. Try to complete the task with a different data set - that often helps find trouble spots.
When you submit your final report, you may remove index.qmd from the _quarto.yml file, which will remove this chapter (which is quite unnecessary for your user) from the report. You should also take the time to make sure your name is listed as both author and copyright holder in the _quarto.yml file. Feel free to make a custom cover for your manual if you would like to do so. You can also tweak the CSS/theme for the book, so long as you’re conscious of accessibility concerns.






Building the report



If you are using RStudio to complete this report, you can hit (Ctrl/Cmd)-Shift-B to build the whole report. You can also type quarto render . on the command line in the project folder to accomplish the same thing.
If you have questions about how to customize or debug your book, it may be helpful to start at the quarto book documentation (positpbcCreatingBook2024?).
crumbs: Assignment Flow

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
4.1 Step 1: Create an Isolated Environment
Think of this as a clean room for your project — packages installed here will not interfere with anything else on your system.
# Create the environment (do this once)
conda create -n autoencoder-env python=3.10
conda activate autoencoder-env
If you do not have conda, use venv:
python3.10 -m venv autoencoder-env
source autoencoder-env/bin/activate      # Mac or Linux
autoencoder-env\Scripts\activate         # Windows

text:
4.2 Step 2: Install Packages
Choose either PyTorch or Keras — you do not need both. PyTorch is more flexible; Keras is simpler to read.
PyTorch (CPU only — works on any machine):
pip install torch==2.2.0 --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu

Keras / TensorFlow:

On a Mac with an M1/M2/M3 chip?



Standard TensorFlow will not install. Use this instead:

pip install tensorflow-metal

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
4.3 Step 3: Save Your Package Versions
After installing, run this to create a record of exactly what you installed:
pip freeze > requirements.txt
Anyone else reproducing your work just runs pip install -r requirements.txt and gets the exact same setup.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
4.4 Step 4: Set Random Seeds
Neural networks use randomness in several places (weight initialization, data shuffling). Without fixing the seed, you will get slightly different results every run. Put this at the very top of your script:
PyTorch:
import random, numpy as np, torch

SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)
torch.manual_seed(SEED)
Keras:
import os, random, numpy as np, tensorflow as tf

SEED = 42
os.environ['PYTHONHASHSEED'] = str(SEED)
random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)
tf.random.set_seed(SEED)
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
section: 5.1 What the Data Must Look Like
text:
5.1 What the Data Must Look Like

All numeric: Neural networks cannot handle text categories directly. Convert them to numbers first (e.g., one-hot encoding).
No missing values: Fill in any gaps before training. Simple mean imputation is fine to start.
All on a similar scale: A column ranging from 0–1 alongside a column ranging from 0–100,000 will confuse the network. Scale everything first.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
5.2 Scaling Your Data
Scale everything to the range [0, 1] before training.






Scale using training data only



Split your data into training and validation sets first, then scale. If you scale the whole dataset before splitting, information from the validation set leaks into training — a common mistake.


from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# Split first
X_train, X_val = train_test_split(X, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)

# Then scale — fit on training data only
scaler  = MinMaxScaler()
X_train = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_val   = scaler.transform(X_val)   # apply the same scaling, do NOT refit
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
6.1 Choosing the Bottleneck Size
The bottleneck size — how many compressed dimensions to use — is the main design decision. Unlike PCA, there is no scree plot to guide you. The approach is to try several values and compare.

Start with 2, 4, 8, and 16
Look for the point where adding more dimensions stops reducing the validation loss much
Choose the smallest size that still gives useful results for your downstream task

See Chapter 9 for a full example of how to search.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
6.3 Building the Model in Keras
from tensorflow import keras
from tensorflow.keras import layers

def build_autoencoder(input_dim, bottleneck_dim):
    inputs  = keras.Input(shape=(input_dim,))
    encoded = layers.Dense(64, activation="relu")(inputs)
    z       = layers.Dense(bottleneck_dim, name="bottleneck")(encoded)
    decoded = layers.Dense(64, activation="relu")(z)
    outputs = layers.Dense(input_dim, activation="sigmoid")(decoded)

    autoencoder = keras.Model(inputs, outputs)
    encoder     = keras.Model(inputs, z)   # for extracting representations later
    return autoencoder, encoder






What is ReLU?



ReLU is a simple rule: if the number coming in is positive, pass it through unchanged; if it is negative, output zero instead. It is the standard choice for hidden layers in modern networks because it is fast and trains reliably.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
7.1 What Training Means
Training means repeatedly showing the network your data and nudging it slightly each time to make the reconstruction better. The measure of how bad the reconstruction is at any moment is called the loss. For our purposes, loss is the average squared difference between the original and the reconstructed data — lower is better.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
7.2 Training in PyTorch
import torch
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset

X_train_t = torch.tensor(X_train, dtype=torch.float32)
X_val_t   = torch.tensor(X_val,   dtype=torch.float32)
loader    = DataLoader(TensorDataset(X_train_t), batch_size=64, shuffle=True)

model     = Autoencoder(input_dim=X_train.shape[1], bottleneck_dim=8)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_fn   = torch.nn.MSELoss()

train_losses, val_losses = [], []

for epoch in range(100):
    model.train()
    for (batch,) in loader:
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss = loss_fn(model(batch), batch)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        train_losses.append(loss_fn(model(X_train_t), X_train_t).item())
        val_losses.append(loss_fn(model(X_val_t),     X_val_t).item())

    if (epoch + 1) % 10 == 0:
        print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}: train={train_losses[-1]:.4f}, val={val_losses[-1]:.4f}")
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
7.4 Reading the Training Plot
Always plot your losses after training. This is the main diagnostic tool you have — think of it as your residual plot.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.plot(train_losses, label="Training loss")
plt.plot(val_losses,   label="Validation loss")
plt.xlabel("Epoch")
plt.ylabel("Reconstruction loss")
plt.legend()
plt.show()
Here is what different patterns mean:

Both lines go down and level off close together → Training worked. You are done.
Training keeps falling but validation levels off or rises → Overfitting. Reduce the number of epochs or use early stopping.
Both lines barely move → The model is not learning. Try more epochs or a smaller learning rate.
Lines bounce around erratically → Learning rate is too high. Try lr=1e-4.







Use early stopping so you do not have to guess the number of epochs



Keras (built-in):
early_stop = keras.callbacks.EarlyStopping(
    monitor="val_loss", patience=15, restore_best_weights=True
)
history = autoencoder.fit(..., callbacks=[early_stop])
PyTorch (add inside the epoch loop):
if val_loss < best_val_loss:
    best_val_loss = val_loss
    torch.save(model.state_dict(), "best_model.pt")
    patience_counter = 0
else:
    patience_counter += 1
    if patience_counter >= 15:
        print("Stopping early")
        break
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
8.1 Visualizing the Compressed Space
If your bottleneck has more than 2 dimensions, reduce to 2D for plotting. It is perfectly fine to use PCA on top of the autoencoder output just for visualization:
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

Z_2d = PCA(n_components=2).fit_transform(Z_train)

plt.scatter(Z_2d[:, 0], Z_2d[:, 1], alpha=0.4, s=10)
plt.xlabel("Component 1")
plt.ylabel("Component 2")
plt.title("Autoencoder latent space (2D projection)")
plt.show()
Color the points by a known grouping variable to see whether meaningful clusters have been separated — a useful check recommended by researchers working on high-dimensional data visualization (Cook, Laa, and Valencia 2018).






The compressed dimensions do not have labels



Unlike PCA components (which you can rank by variance explained), the bottleneck dimensions have no natural ordering or interpretation. Judge the result by whether it helps your downstream task, not by what the individual numbers mean.
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians
text:
6.3 Set Random Seeds
Put this at the top of every script so results are reproducible:
PyTorch:
import random, numpy as np, torch
random.seed(42); np.random.seed(42); torch.manual_seed(42)
torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True
torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False
Keras:
import os, random, numpy as np, tensorflow as tf
os.environ['PYTHONHASHSEED'] = '42'
random.seed(42); np.random.seed(42); tf.random.set_seed(42)
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
5.1 Requirements
Your data must meet these conditions before training:







Requirement
Why




All numeric
Neural networks cannot handle raw categories or text


No missing values
A single missing value will break training


Scaled to [0, 1]
Features on very different scales confuse the network


At least a few thousand rows
Small datasets rarely benefit from autoencoders
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
5.2 Encoding Categorical Variables
import pandas as pd

# One-hot encode categorical columns
df = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=["species", "region"])
X  = df.values
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
5.3 Handling Missing Values
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer

imputer = SimpleImputer(strategy="mean")
X = imputer.fit_transform(X)
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians
text:
5.4 Scaling






Always split your data before scaling. If you scale the full dataset first, information from the validation set leaks into training — a very common mistake.



from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X_train, X_val = train_test_split(X, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)

scaler  = MinMaxScaler()
X_train = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_val   = scaler.transform(X_val)     # apply only — do NOT refit on validation
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Practical Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians
text:
8.1 PyTorch
import torch, torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset

class Autoencoder(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, k):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(input_dim, 32), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(32, k))
        self.decoder = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(k, 32), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(32, input_dim), nn.Sigmoid())
    def forward(self, x): return self.decoder(self.encoder(x))
    def encode(self, x):  return self.encoder(x)

X_tr  = torch.tensor(X_train, dtype=torch.float32)
X_vl  = torch.tensor(X_val,   dtype=torch.float32)
model = Autoencoder(input_dim=4, k=2)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_fn   = nn.MSELoss()
train_losses, val_losses = [], []

for epoch in range(200):
    model.train()
    for (batch,) in DataLoader(TensorDataset(X_tr), batch_size=32, shuffle=True):
        optimizer.zero_grad(); loss = loss_fn(model(batch), batch)
        loss.backward(); optimizer.step()
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        train_losses.append(loss_fn(model(X_tr), X_tr).item())
        val_losses.append(loss_fn(model(X_vl), X_vl).item())
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians
text:
8.2 Keras
from tensorflow import keras
from tensorflow.keras import layers

def build(input_dim, k):
    inp = keras.Input(shape=(input_dim,))
    x   = layers.Dense(32, activation="relu")(inp)
    z   = layers.Dense(k, name="bottleneck")(x)
    x   = layers.Dense(32, activation="relu")(z)
    out = layers.Dense(input_dim, activation="sigmoid")(x)
    return keras.Model(inp, out), keras.Model(inp, z)

autoencoder, encoder = build(input_dim=4, k=2)
autoencoder.compile(optimizer="adam", loss="mse")
autoencoder.fit(X_train, X_train, epochs=200, batch_size=32,
                validation_data=(X_val, X_val))
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians

Minima.” Neural Networks 2 (1): 53–58.

Autoencoders.” https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.05908.

Learning. MIT Press.

the Dimensionality of Data with Neural Networks.”
Science 313 (5786): 504–7. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1127647.


Nalisnick, Eric, Padhraic Smyth, and Dustin Tran. 2023. “A Brief
Tour of Deep Learning from a Statistical Perspective.” Annual
Review of Statistics and Its Application 10: 219–46. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-statistics-032921-013738.

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians
text:
3.1 Knowledge

PCA — autoencoders are a nonlinear extension of PCA, familiarity with PCA is essential to continue
Basic Python — reading and running scripts is sufficent, no deep learning experience needed
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians
text:
3.2 Software
Choose one deep learning library, you do not need both:

PyTorch — more flexible, popular in research
Keras — simpler syntax, faster to get started

You will also need Python 3.10 and conda (Miniconda from docs.conda.io).
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians

title: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians
text:
4.1 Should You Use an Autoencoder?
Use PCA if: your dataset is small, you need interpretable components, or you want something fast with no training required.
Use an autoencoder if: PCA reconstructions look poor, your data has complex structure (images, sensor readings, expressions), or you have at least a few thousand rows (Hinton and Salakhutdinov 2006).






Tip



When in doubt, try PCA first. If it works well enough, use it. Autoencoders require more setup and tuning.





Table 4.1: Comparison of PCA and autoencoders






PCA
Autoencoder




Finds nonlinear patterns
No
Yes


Output usable in a model
Yes
Yes


Needs training
No
Yes


Components have meaning
Yes
No


Works on images / audio
Poorly
Yes
crumbs: 1  Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians
